rationales.self_direction: He says he built the company to "do things my way," making personal choice the core frame for this situation.
rationales.power: He "held back" and let Anna present instead of changing the deck just to leave his "fingerprints," which means giving up control.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Anna's deck "solid work" and lets her present it, backing a colleague's success instead of centering himself.

rationales.self_direction: He dismisses the association's advice in favor of "actual decisions that affect actual outcomes," showing a strong preference to act on his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: He openly rejects the meeting's "standard recommendations for standard companies" and then "left early," breaking the expected script.

rationales.self_direction: He keeps coming back to not being able to "stomach the idea of someone else making decisions about my work again" and values knowing "I chose it."
rationales.security: He contrasts Marcus's offer with "stability, predictability, a clear path to retirement" and still leans away from it, choosing uncertainty over safety.

rationales.conformity: He says his first instinct was to "push back hard" but he "didn't" and let Erik handle the diplomacy, restraining himself to keep things smooth.
rationales.tradition: When Emilia mentions the "Lucia dinner tomorrow," he says yes and joins the holiday observance instead of skipping it.
rationales.benevolence: He frames the choice as keeping the "client's happy, team's intact," prioritizing relationship maintenance over venting his frustration.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects Marcus's offer because what he wants is "to keep making choices" and to face problems that are "problems I chose."
rationales.security: He declines the safer offer and even says Sara "wanted more certainty than I could give her," showing he keeps choosing a less secure path.
rationales.benevolence: He closes the office by handing out "bonuses" and is moved by Anna's tears and Erik's toast, showing real care for his team.

rationales.achievement: The postdoc presents his framework with "my slides" and "my structure," yet he "said nothing," letting his own work go uncredited.
rationales.power: He watches someone else claim "something I've been working on" and does not challenge it, surrendering status and control in the room.
He says the best conversations are the ones where "nobody's positioning themselves" or measuring publications and funding, explicitly preferring life away from status competition.

rationales.self_direction: He thinks his original framing is stronger but still restructures the chapter because "you have to write for the audience that matters," yielding to external demands.
rationales.hedonism: He spends the evening with "eyes burning," a messy flat, and "bread and cheese" after not cooking for three days, clearly sacrificing comfort.
rationales.achievement: He keeps reorganizing the chapter around reviewer expectations and pushes through exhaustion to strengthen the journal submission.
He is willing to adjust citations and play the journal game because it could get him "a better review at the European Journal of Political Economy."
rationales.power: He admits "you learn not to give away the full picture" when spots are limited, using information strategically in a competitive hierarchy.
He says he cannot become "another doctoral student waiting for permission" and is already thinking about what kind of "leverage" is needed to make things happen.
rationales.universalism: He prepared a proposal to give "doctoral candidates more presenting time," pushing for a fairer distribution of opportunity beyond his own immediate gain.

rationales.self_direction: When the committee stalls him, his response is to "find another route" because "standing still isn't really something I do well."
rationales.achievement: He assembled the application with an updated CV, polished statement, and letter, then immediately started looking for ways to keep the opportunity alive.

rationales.power: He openly defends being the one who runs meetings, reads everything first, and "know[s] how to organise people or push things forward."
rationales.conformity: He refuses to shrink himself to match "expectations of what a doctoral student should be" and is comfortable "tak[ing] up more space" than expected.

rationales.self_direction: He has a "clear view" on the right cases but ends up with "Her cases it is," unable to follow his own judgment.
rationales.power: Lena is "firm" and Bjorn's silence means "she won," leaving him to absorb the consequences without influence over the decision.

rationales.universalism: His hesitation centers on "cutting corners on something that goes to people who cannot complain if it breaks," showing concern for vulnerable end users over convenience.

rationales.security: The Dubai salary could cover "both girls' tuition in one year" and relieve family strain, but he still keeps choosing the less stable path.
rationales.universalism: He closes the job listing while thinking about why "clean water matters" and the families in Azraq, keeping the mission above an easier, better-paid role.
The work is framed around getting filters out, and Abu Faris's story that "the kids stopped getting sick last winter" shows the broader human impact driving the effort.

rationales.conformity: He keeps the Bekaa idea to himself because he has "learned not to pitch things until I have run the numbers," submitting to the board's expectations instead of speaking freely.

rationales.universalism: He is consumed by the fact that families now have to buy "trucked water at five times the cost" and starts calculating how many households are affected.

rationales.conformity: He says "yes to all of it" - logos, photos, and executive visits - because the funding requires going along with the sponsor's script.
rationales.universalism: Even while taking the deal, he keeps centering that "Fifty units is fifty families with clean water" and rejects treating camp residents as "props."

rationales.security: His daughter asks why they do not live in a bigger house, and he admits the uncertainty of whether the work will outlast "the next funding cycle," yet he stays with it.
rationales.universalism: He explains the business in terms of filters, camps, and how water-borne illness keeps kids out of school, showing the wider welfare aim at the center of his choices.
Even in a grim overtime shift, the work is about finishing the Zarqa batch - "Fifty units, all sealed and loaded" - for people who need the filters.

rationales.power: He dismisses Samir's promotion by saying "Samir's title is a line on a business card" and rejects measuring success by "Cars, titles" and schools.
rationales.universalism: What feels "real" to him is that a mother reported her son's eczema cleared after the filter, showing he values concrete social impact over status.
